hero.title: Glyph: A Body for AI
hero.oneLiner: I built a wearable test bed to see what happens when AI leaves the chat window and becomes something you can wear, move, and build on.

context.text: Glyph started in Northwestern's Spring 2026 Product Practicum. My first idea was a wearable test bed for tinkerers. I spoke with people who build things about attachment, feedback, and adding new parts. At the same time, I kept thinking about how AI was getting better while we were still talking to it through a chat box. Glyph brought those two ideas together.

bullets: I built one model to work out the shape, materials, lights, sleeve, and side module.
The clear model uses thermoformed PETG, PETG-GF parts, a black acrylic layer, and three white light filaments.
I built the working demo separately with a Waveshare ESP32-S3 Touch LCD 1.83-inch board and a XIAO nRF52840 Sense Plus.
I got IMU gestures, BLE-HID, air mouse control, telemetry, Wi-Fi and BLE bridges, and Home Assistant lighting control working.
The demo let me point, select, change brightness and color, and confirm with a snap or tap.

decisions.why: Talking to people who build wearables gave me clear needs: easy attachment, room for new parts, and feedback that makes sense quickly.
decisions.tradeoff: I could not build the full system in one quarter.

decisions.decision: Use Nothing, the company, as the class reference
decisions.why: I liked the clear shells, black and white hardware, useful lights, and the way the products show how they are put together.
decisions.tradeoff: This was my class project. Nothing was only used as a reference and was not involved.

decisions.decision: Build the form and the working demo separately
decisions.why: One model let me make the wearable look right. The other let me test whether the gestures and controls worked.
decisions.tradeoff: The polished shell and the working electronics were not one device.

worked: The conversations with tinkerers made the module and connection ideas much clearer.
Picking Nothing helped me make consistent choices about the shell, lights, and visible parts.
The two models let me show both the physical idea and the working controls.
didnt: The polished model and the working electronics were not combined.
Haptics, EMG, a dense light matrix, a custom PCB, and the full set of modules were not built.
The size and sleeve still need longer wear testing.

outcome.text: For the final, I brought in the polished wearable model and the separate electronics demo. I kept them separate because that was the honest state of the project.
outcome.proof: Two physical models and a live gesture-to-room demo, Spring 2026

reflection.text: AI has enough brains. I wanted to see what happens when you give it a body.

skills.label: Human-Centered Design
skills.description: I spoke with tinkerers about attaching parts, reading feedback, and changing wearable devices.

skills.label: Industrial Design
skills.description: I designed the gauntlet shape, clear shell, sleeve, lights, and side module.

skills.label: Physical Prototyping
skills.description: I used separate models for the finished form and the working controls.

skills.label: Embedded Systems
skills.description: I worked with ESP32-S3 and nRF52840 hardware, IMU input, Wi-Fi, BLE, telemetry, and room controls.

skills.label: Interaction Design
skills.description: I built the gesture flow for selecting, changing, and confirming an action.

skills.label: Brand Study
skills.description: Nothing, the company, was used as the class reference for the clear shell, useful lights, and black and white hardware.
